id: 99 Consulting - Culture Review and Training Design - Financial Regulator, Hong Kong
title: Culture Review and Training Design for a Major Financial Regulator in Hong Kong
description: The Client is an independent non-governmental statutory regulatory body in Hong Kong with more than 500 employees.  During the process of implementing various improvement initiatives in the past few years, it was identified that there were improvement opportunities in cross-division collaboration, improving service quality of the organization and in enhancing employee communication and engagement.  The Client therefore invited PwC to provide consultancy services to review the current culture, articulate the desired future culture and facilitate the cascading of the new culture.
activities: Reviewed the current corporate culture in relation to the people behavior, internal communication, employee engagement and perception to change etc.
Assisted the development of the desired corporate culture and its alignment and consensus building at the senior management level
Facilitated commitments to behavior change at the corporate, departmental and individual levels
Designed a culture training program to promote the desired culture and behavior among its employees
benefits: Reached consensus about the current culture and articulated the existing behavior to be changed as well as the desired behavior to be practiced
Developed clear vision, mission and values for the Client for cascading and communication
Designed and delivered culture cascading workshops to middle management level and advised on other supportive mechanism to roll out the new culture and reinforce the new behavior
source_file: 99 Consulting - Culture Review and Training Design - Financial Regulator, Hong Kong.pptx

id: 98 Consulting - JV Partnership Termination and close down - Heavy Industry, China
title: Facilitate an Australia based hanger processor to terminate the sorting and warehousing facility with its JV partner in China, Shanghai
description: As a strategic move to improve operating efficiency and cost saving, our client was terminating its JV operation in Shanghai and set up a new WOFE in other location. The key challenges faced by the client included the negotiation of the Termination Agreement with the JV partner in a cooperative and friendly manner, and ensure the smooth transformation of all assets to the new WOFE, mass lay off of 300 employees and terminate 3 lease contracts in a harmonious and cost effective way. Given the lack of relevant experience and resources in China, PwC was engaged to review and comment on the negotiation conditions and provide necessary resources and support in monitoring the implementation of the agreed conditions within the Termination Agreement.
activities: Collected, reviewed and commented on the Leases Contracts and Assets Transfer & Payment Schedule
Developed Termination Check list to facilitate the negotiation
Followed up with the negotiation results and advised on the settlement agreement
Bridged the communication gap between both parties to ensure on-time mutual understanding
On-site facilitation to manage both internal and external communication with employees, government Officials and other stakeholders
Reviewed, commented and oversaw on the finalization of retrenchment package between two parties
Established the assets transfer checking process for FGs, WIP and others
On-site daily checking of original delivery documents and certificate for various assets transfer
benefits: Smooth asset transfer completed in 4 weeks with on-time settlement of payment
150 containers, 300 individual customer orders processed and 4 leases terminated
300 employees harmoniously downsized in 1 month with government supports
No strikes, or disruptions, potential antagonistic environment and managed in a harmonious manner
source_file: 98 Consulting - JV Partnership Termination and close down - Heavy Industry, China.pptx

title: Relocation – Strategic People Planning (SPP) for a Boiler Manufacturer Based in a Large Second Tier City in China
description: The energy division of a large European conglomerate had acquired a Chinese State Owned Enterprise, leader in the local boiler manufacturing business. After a successful integration, the headquarter of the European conglomerate decided to upgrade the manufacturing facilities of their new acquisition in order to build more, cheaper and better boilers with the intention to target the Asian market. PwC personnel was engaged to plan the relocation of the 2,000 employees working in the factory.
activities: The SPP facilitated effective project management of the transfer and hiring of all employees throughout the different stages of the relocation. Incorporating all key project milestones, this flexible and highly specialized model enabled PwC Personnel to plan all activities and costs for the identification, assessment and placement of all required staff which would roll out during the relocation phase and the subsequent ramp-up stage to reach a total workforce of 3,500 employees over the ensuing 5 years. All timings had been linked to the key relocation events such as: relocation of the equipments, commissioning of the new equipments, start date of production, etc. The client was also able to achieve flexibility for changing circumstances through using ‘What if’ scenarios.

Good community relations dictated that a considerable number of employees be recruited locally. A large effort has been done on the sourcing and recruitment of identified key rare skills talents. More backward time has been allowed for their recruitment process to ensure on-time and on-requirements on-boarding.
benefits: During the intensive 100 days project, PwC Personnel structured a fully loaded SPP - a flexible and detailed project plan which covered every aspect of each employment event over the 10 years that the client required for the full set up, including the introduction of training programmes in the educational institutions for future requirements. The client was able to then use this tool on an ongoing basis.

title: Analysis of Post Acquisition Salesforce Integration for a U.S. Listed Food Company in Asia
description: A U.S. company was facing integration challenges, having acquired a division from its key competitor, which had a different culture and leadership style. An initial integration effort showed evidence of disengagement, silos, and ongoing ‘Us’ and ‘Them’ thinking. The client needed an assessment and implementation plan to clearly understand the different regions, channels and the new culture, as well as the growth opportunities.
activities: PwC Personnel conducted a mapping analysis through structured interviews with selected staff.  Special care was taken to ensure representation across regions, levels, and “source” organization.

Assessment of Culture, Leadership / Staff Attitudes - The PwC Personnel team assessed the corporate culture, competencies, attitudes and expectations of selected managers and representative talent. Qualitative comments and important quotations provided “color” to the calibrated scoring and measures.

Focused insights - PwC Personnel identified significant problem areas including some abrasive leaders & managers, internally focused competitive staff, increased training needs and the requirement for better merchandising at the store level so that the outcomes of the assignment were immediately usable to different regions and sales channel leaders.
Detailed Implementation Plan for Transformation - A week-by-week implementation plan was prepared to guide the recommended transformation based on the insights revealed by the mapping study.
benefits: PwC Personnel identified significant management “blind spots” and areas of confusion, particularly in regards to a culture frequently described as “oil and water.” Communication upgrades including a Management in Residence programme and more appropriate means for reaching junior sales staff were recommended. An approach to deliver a “new beginning” with a clearly defined vision and change management events was designed to align the whole sales organization.

id: 95 Consulting - Post Acquisition Talent Assessment - Food Manufacturing, China
title: Post-Acquisition – Talent Assessment for a U.S. Listed Food Company in Asia
description: A US$35bn+, NYSE-listed food major was acquiring a global competitor in one of its key operating divisions and wanted to ensure that the post-deal integration transition was managed by the best internal team. PwC Personnel were engaged to design and implement an “unbiased” talent appraisal of all key personnel in both businesses across all key Asia markets. There were substantial time pressures facing the coordination and successful completion of the assessments, to fit into the existing global integration timetables.
activities: Corporate Culture Differences - One of the challenges facing the client during this period was that the two companies had entirely different and incompatible corporate cultures; central control and process vs. local autonomy and empowerment. PwC Personnel determined not only those individuals who had the most value to offer in the local markets, but also those leaders who understood the challenges of implementing successful change.

Uncovering Local Talent - PwC Personnel conducted approximately 60 detailed assessment interviews across both client and target organizations.  A common problem facing acquisitive companies is that talent often exits the organization and the “wrong” people are retained.
benefits: During this project PwC Personnel were able to identify a new generation of future leaders inside both companies and highlight them to client’s regional leadership, to ensure that full efforts were made to retain and develop them. As a result of the Assessment, client was able to appoint the best team to lead what was a highly effective integration and transition programme.
source_file: 95 Consulting - Post Acquisition Talent Assessment - Food Manufacturing, China.pptx

title: Negotiation Support with a Building Products Company in China
description: The client had been negotiating with a Chinese company for some time without making any real progress. PwC Personnel were engaged to advise the client on how to push the negotiations forward to a conclusion.
activities: PwC Personnel’s experience and understanding of how to negotiate across different cultures allowed the miscommunications to be clarified and ensured that the key issues were addressed effectively.
Communications were established at various levels in order to achieve transparency.
The PwC Personnel team was also able to stop the loss of intellectual property which had been occurring.
The evidence gained in the negotiation phase, gave the client the impetus to set up on their own rather than proceed with a joint venture.
benefits: PwC Personnel were able to bring the negotiations to a conclusive point within three months. The client decided to decline the deal for valid reasons.

id: 72 Search - Multi Team Localisation - Aerospace, Asia and M East
title: Talent Mapping for a leader in the Telecommunications industry in Asia
description:
An iconic Fortune 500 company with over 50% of sales in Asia had no Asian senior management. The global CEO wanted to change the company’s ethnocentric culture and bring in leaders capable of running the company in the next 4 - 7 years.
PwC Personnel was engaged to identify stars able to enter the company, absorb its complex culture and be taken through a rapid promotion process in order to become leaders of business units.
activities: PwC Personnel conducted 22 high level searches in 8 countries across 6 global business units. A high profile campaign to gain the attention of suitable candidates was conducted employing advertisements in high profile print media both local and international, viral e-mail and extensive executive search. Stars then had to be persuaded that they could progress in a company with a poor reputation for xenophobia.

Conversely PwC Personnel had to deal with the Client’s internal issues. Highly individualistic units were resistant to any central initiatives and there was a deep rooted perception that future leadership was defined in western terms. The PwC Personnel team helped the Client to develop a programme to ensure the incoming stars could be absorbed and a good deal of time was spent educating them to value and respect Asian leadership with its differences in style.
benefits: The programme was implemented and internal opposition was overcome. High performers were successfully identified and hired and are now rising through the ranks.
source_file: 72 Search - Multi Team Localisation - Aerospace, Asia and M East.pptx

id: 28 Search - Team - Agricultural Commodity Group, China and SE Asia
title: Multi-Hiring for a leader in the Agricultural Commodity Trading industry in North & South-East Asia
description:
A leading Australian agricultural commodity trading group was restructuring their Asia operations, splitting their regional HQ into two offices located in China and South-east Asia.
PwC Personnel was engaged to assist in the strategic appointment of key regional and marketing managers who were to be based locally in Asia.   The specific nature of the search required the PwC Personnel project team to develop the technical skills to be able to interrogate candidates’ skills to a very deep level in order to fully assess their ability.
activities: PwC Personnel worked closely with the company’s project team, which gave them an understanding of company’s strategic plans, corporate cultural issues and current challenges to ensure the best qualified candidates were brought forward.

The project team conducted an exhaustive search of the industry, contacting hundreds of people in the agricultural trading industry in order to find the top talent within the industry who could drive through real changes and lead the company in their next stage of growth.

The search covered eight countries in the region, as well as an internal search within the Client’s company to ensure that potential candidates were not overlooked.   The multi-country, multi-position nature of the search demanded the ability to overcome logistical challenges within a strict timeframe, as well as the ability to provide a consistent approach to the interview process in order to provide a “level playing field” for all candidates.

As with many Australian companies in Asia, this company was confronted by the challenge of localizing very senior positions. PwC Personnel’s processes met this challenge successfully and filled the majority of positions with locally based senior executives, and negotiated local packages for all incumbents.
benefits: PwC Personnel successfully managed the Client’s valuable time and resources to source candidates and negotiate contracts on their behalf.  The transition of the employees into the newly established offices was very smooth and the company’s operations in the region are benefiting as a result.
source_file: 28 Search - Team - Agricultural Commodity Group, China and SE Asia.pptx

id: 44 Search - Regional Dir - Logistics, China and SE Asia
title: Executive Search for Logistics Infrastructure & Services in North & South East Asia
description: A global leader in the pallet-pooling industry was reviewing their strategic direction with a view to redefining themselves in the intercontinental arena.
activities: Good Understanding of Company - PwC personnel worked with the client in order to closely understand the company’s strategic plans, corporate culture and growth challenges in order to identify and bring forward the best-qualified candidates. Utilizing a multi-channel methodology of advertising, proactive executive search, database search and viral marketing, PwC personnel identified key players and hidden talent within the industry. Deep logistics skills and Asia knowledge were not in their own sufficient to ensure the successful integration of the candidate into this role. The role also required that the appointee had a strong track record within the pooling industry, and preferably a further understanding of the IBC markets.

Transparent Regional Leadership - This industry knowledge needed to be coupled with sound leadership and business development capabilities as well as the transparency and communication skills to be able to work with remote headquarters.
benefits: PwC personnel rapidly identified a candidate who would be able to deliver innovative solutions to the organizational and growth challenges faced by the client as it underwent its revitalization.
source_file: 44 Search - Regional Dir - Logistics, China and SE Asia.pptx

id: 79 Search - CFO Pre-Ipo - Manufac., China, Japan and Taiwan
title: Executive Search for Integrated Circuits in China, Taiwan and Japan
description: A rapidly growing local integrated circuits manufacturer was moving towards a listing but did not fully comprehend the complexity that this entailed. In addition, the local Private Equity group behind the company had no US listing experience.
activities: Large-Scale CFO Search - In order to locate the right candidate the PwC personnel identified the CFOs of every Chinese company (including Taiwanese ones) that had listed on a foreign stock exchange, along with their immediate subordinates. The search also extended to Japan.
Culture - Interviews were conducted to find which of these potential candidates possessed the right culture for the company and would be able to communicate with Wall Street.

Candidate Integration - Since the company had not had any changes in senior management in 25 years, PwC personnel also had to work with the management team to ensure they would be open and accepting of a new team member of this calibre.
benefits: A suitable candidate was found and the company is now in the process of listing.
source_file: 79 Search - CFO Pre-Ipo - Manufac., China, Japan and Taiwan.pptx
